author: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon
book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

lineage: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon.
The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

metadata.author: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon

title: Canon XX.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: It shall not be lawful, as we have already decreed, for clergymen officiating in one church to be appointed to the church of another city, but they shall cleave to that in which they were first thought worthy to minister; those, however, being excepted, who have been driven by necessity from their own country, and have therefore removed to another church.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: And if, after this decree, any bishop shall receive a clergyman belonging to another bishop, it is decreed that both the received and the receiver shall be excommunicated until such time as the clergyman who has removed shall have returned to his own church.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: A clergyman of one city shall not be given a cure in another.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: But if he has been driven from his native place and shall go into another he shall be without blame.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: If any bishop receives clergymen from without his diocese he shall be excommunicated as well as the cleric he receives.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: It is quite doubtful as to what “excommunication” means in this canon, probably not anathematism (so think the commentators) but separation from the communion of the other bishops, and suspension from the performance of clerical functions.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: This canon is the third of those which were originally proposed by Marcian in the end of the sixth session, as certain articles for which synodical sanction was desirable (see above Canons iij. and iv.).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: It was after they had been delivered by the Emperor’s own hand to Anatolius of Constantinople that the Council broke out into plaudits, one of which is sufficiently startling, τῷ ἱερεῖ, τῷ βασιλεῖ (Mansi, vii., 177).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The imperial draft is in this case very slightly altered.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: A reference is made to a previous determination (i.e., canon x.) against clerical pluralities, and it is ordered that “clerics registered as belonging to one church shall not be ranked as belonging to the church of another city, but must be content with the one in which they were originally admitted to minister, excepting those who, having lost their own country, have been compelled to migrate to another church,”—an exception intelligible enough at such a period.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Eleven years before, the Vandal Gaiseric had expelled the Catholic bishops and priests of Western Africa from their churches: Quodvultdeus, bishop of Carthage with many of his clergy, had been “placed on board some unseaworthy vessels,” and yet, “by the Divine mercy, had been carried safe to Naples” (Vict.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Vandal., i., 5: he mentions other bishops as driven into exile).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Somewhat later, the surge of the Hunnish invasion had frightened the bishop of Sirmium into sending his church vessels to Attila’s Gaulish secretary and had swept onward in 447 to within a short distance of the “New Rome” (Hodgkin, Italy and her Invaders, ii., 54–56).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: And the very year of the Council was the most momentous in the whole history of the “Barbaric” movement.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The bishops who assembled in October at Chalcedon must have heard by that time of the massacre of the Metz clergy on Easter Eve, of a bishop of Rheims slain at his own altar, of the deliverance of Orleans at the prayer of St. Anianus, of “the supreme battle” in the plain of Chalons, which turned back Attila and rescued Christian Gaul (Hodgkin, ii., 129–152; Kitchin, Hist.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: This canon is found in the Corpus Juris Canonici, Gratian’s Decretum, Pars I., Dist. lxxi, c. iv.
